title: The Danger of Drifting and the Incarnate Son

sections.header: Verses 1–4 — "How Shall We Escape If We Ignore So Great a Salvation?"

heb.gloss: to drift away, to slip away
heb.paragraph: The verb παραρρέω (pararreō) in the passive means ‘to be carried past’ or ‘to drift away’—like a boat slipping its moorings or a ring sliding off a finger. The imagery is not violent apostasy but gradual, almost imperceptible departure. This is the first of five warning passages in Hebrews (2:1–4; 3:7–4:13; 5:11–6:12; 10:26–31; 12:25–29) that punctuate the theological argument with urgent pastoral exhortation.

refs.note: Paul’s reference to the law being ‘given through angels’ confirms the widespread Jewish tradition that angels mediated the Sinai covenant—the assumption underlying the comparison here.
The fourth warning passage intensifies the language of judgment: if we deliberately keep on sinning after receiving knowledge of the truth, no sacrifice for sins is left.
The fifth warning passage returns to the comparison: if those who refused the one who warned on earth did not escape, how much less will we escape if we turn away from the one who warns from heaven.

notes.note: We must pay the most careful attention to what we have heard, so that we do not drift away from it. Spiritual decline is usually gradual, not sudden—a slow drift, not a dramatic departure.

If the law given through angels was binding, and every violation received its just punishment, how shall we escape if we ignore so great a salvation? This salvation was first announced by the Lord, confirmed by those who heard him, and attested by God himself through signs, wonders, miracles, and gifts of the Holy Spirit.

The first warning is framed as an argument from lesser to greater: if the angelic law carried penalties for disobedience, how much more terrible the consequence of neglecting the salvation proclaimed by the Son himself. The danger is not dramatic rebellion but careless drifting—a warning as relevant today as when it was written.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The verb παραρυῶμεν (drift away) carries connotations of carelessness rather than intentional departure—the danger is neglect, not active rebellion.
The phrase 'message spoken through angels' reflects the Jewish tradition of angelic mediation at Sinai, attested in Deut 33:2 LXX, Acts 7:38, 53, and Gal 3:19.

lane.source: William Lane, WBC Hebrews — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The first warning passage employs a standard rabbinical qal wa-homer (light to heavy) argument: if the Torah given through angelic intermediaries carried strict sanctions, how much more the salvation announced by the Son himself. The nautical metaphor παραρρέω (to drift past) suggests unintentional departure—the community’s danger is not flagrant apostasy but gradual erosion of commitment through inattention.

cockerill.source: Gareth Cockerill, NICNT Hebrews — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The warning is carefully calibrated: the drifting imagery (παραρυῶμεν) suggests a community not in open revolt but in slow spiritual decline. The three-stage authentication of salvation—announced by the Lord, confirmed by eyewitnesses, attested by God through the Spirit’s gifts—creates an unbreakable chain of testimony that makes neglect inexcusable. The rhetorical question (‘how shall we escape?’) expects no answer: there is no escape.

hist.historical: Hebrews was written to Jewish Christians facing pressure to abandon their faith and return to Judaism. The destruction of the Jerusalem temple (AD 70) loomed or had just occurred, removing the visible center of Jewish worship. The author argues that Christ fulfills and supersedes all Old Testament institutions — priesthood, sacrifice, covenant, and tabernacle. The recipients faced persecution (10:32-34), some were drifting away (2:1), and the letter urges perseverance by demonstrating Christ's superiority to angels, Moses, Joshua, and the Levitical priesthood. The sophisticated Greek style and extensive use of the Septuagint suggest a Hellenistic Jewish-Christian author addressing a similarly educated audience, possibly in Rome (13:24).
hist.context: The first warning passage interrupts the angel argument with a lesser-to-greater (qal wa-homer) comparison: if the message delivered through angels (the Torah, cf. Gal 3:19; Acts 7:53) carried binding authority with just penalties, how much more serious is neglecting the salvation announced by the Lord himself. This rhetorical structure—theological exposition followed by practical exhortation—will repeat throughout Hebrews and reflects the author’s homiletical purpose (13:22, ‘my word of exhortation’).

sections.header: Verses 5–18 — "He Was Made Lower Than the Angels for a Little While"

heb.paragraph: The noun ἀρχηγός (archēgos, from ἀρχή + ἄγω) means one who leads the way, a pioneer or founder. Applied to Jesus as the ἀρχηγός of salvation (v. 10), it portrays him as the trailblazer who opens the path to God through his own suffering. The word recurs in 12:2 (‘pioneer and perfecter of faith’) and appears in Acts 3:15; 5:31. It combines the ideas of origination and leadership: Jesus both creates and leads the way of salvation.

heb.gloss: to make perfect, to bring to completion
heb.paragraph: The verb τελειόω (teleioō) is central to Hebrews’ theology: it appears 9 times in the epistle (more than in all other NT books combined). It does not mean moral perfection but vocational completion—being brought to the goal (τέλος) of one’s calling. Jesus was ‘made perfect’ through suffering (v. 10)—not morally improved but vocationally qualified to serve as the perfect high priest through experiential solidarity with those he saves.

refs.note: The psalmist’s meditation on human dignity (‘what is man that you are mindful of him?’) is read christologically: the one made ‘a little lower than the angels’ is Jesus, whose temporary humiliation leads to universal sovereignty.
The Christ hymn’s pattern of descent (taking the form of a servant) and ascent (exaltation and the name above every name) parallels the Hebrews 2 argument of incarnation–suffering–glorification.
The author attributes Isa 8:17–18 to Jesus (‘Here am I, and the children God has given me’), identifying Jesus with the prophet’s trust in God and solidarity with his people.

notes.note: God did not subject the coming world to angels but to the Son. Quoting Psalm 8: What is man that you are mindful of him? You made him a little lower than the angels, crowned him with glory and honor, and put everything under his feet. We do not yet see everything subject to him—but we see Jesus.

We see Jesus, who was made lower than the angels for a little while, now crowned with glory and honor because he suffered death, so that by the grace of God he might taste death for everyone. It was fitting that God should make the pioneer of salvation perfect through suffering.

Both the one who makes people holy and those who are made holy are of the same family. So Jesus is not ashamed to call them brothers. He quotes Psalm 22 and Isaiah 8—identifying himself with suffering humanity.

Since the children have flesh and blood, he shared in their humanity so that by his death he might break the power of him who holds the power of death—the devil—and free those who all their lives were held in slavery by their fear of death. He is now a merciful and faithful high priest.

The incarnation is not degradation but strategic condescension. The Son became human to do what angels could never do: die a substitutionary death, destroy the devil’s power, and become a merciful high priest who understands human weakness from personal experience. Every element of Christ’s humiliation serves a redemptive purpose. He calls us brothers—not from obligation but from love.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Psalm 8 was originally about humanity’s God-given dignity and dominion. The author of Hebrews reads it christologically: Jesus is the ‘son of man’ who fulfills the psalm’s vision of universal dominion through incarnation and exaltation.

The phrase 'make the pioneer of salvation perfect through suffering' (τελειῶσαι) does not imply moral deficiency but vocational qualification: suffering equipped Jesus for his role as saving high priest.
The phrase 'merciful and faithful high priest' (ἐλεήμων καὶ πιστός ἀρχιερεύς) introduces for the first time the priestly christology that will dominate chapters 5–10.

lane.source: William Lane, WBC Hebrews — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The incarnation theology of this section answers the question raised by the angel comparison: if the Son is superior to angels, why was he made lower than them? The answer is soteriological: only by sharing humanity’s condition could the Son die on humanity’s behalf. The τελειόω (perfecting) vocabulary introduces a concept unique to Hebrews: the Son’s vocational completion through suffering. The high-priest designation in v. 17 is the hinge between the angel comparison (chs. 1–2) and the priestly exposition (chs. 5–10).

cockerill.source: Gareth Cockerill, NICNT Hebrews — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The section reinterprets Ps 8 christologically: the Son’s temporary lowering below the angels was not defeat but the means by which he achieved universal sovereignty. The sequence—incarnation, suffering, death, glorification—mirrors the pattern of the Christ hymn (Phil 2) but with distinctive Hebrews vocabulary: ἀρχηγός (pioneer), τελειόω (perfect/complete), ἀρχιερεύς (high priest). The brother-language (vv. 11–13) grounds the priestly ministry in relational solidarity: the high priest is not a distant functionary but a family member who shares the human condition from the inside.

hist.context: The author now explains why the Son was temporarily made ‘lower than the angels’ (quoting Ps 8:4–6): the incarnation was not a diminishment but a strategic necessity. By sharing human flesh and blood, the Son could die, and through death destroy the one who holds the power of death—the devil (v. 14). This section introduces key Hebrews concepts: the pioneer of salvation made perfect through suffering (v. 10), the sanctifier and sanctified sharing one origin (v. 11), Jesus as the brother of believers (vv. 11–12), and the merciful and faithful high priest (v. 17)—the last phrase introducing the priestly christology that will dominate chs. 5–10.

themes.note: Mercy dominates through the merciful high priest designation and the solidarity-through-incarnation theology. Judgment surfaces in the warning passage. Sovereignty is maintained even through the humiliation narrative.

rows.label: vv.1–4
rows.text: First warning passage: the danger of drifting

rows.label: vv.5–9
rows.text: Psalm 8 applied to Jesus: temporary humiliation, eternal glory

rows.label: vv.10–13
rows.text: Pioneer of salvation perfected through suffering

rows.label: vv.14–18
rows.text: Incarnation, death-destruction, and merciful high priest

lit.note: Chapter 2 explains the paradox of the Son’s temporary humiliation: he was made lower than the angels in order to die, destroy the devil’s power, and become a merciful high priest who sympathizes with human weakness.

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Danger of Drifting and the Incarnate Son

translations.text: and put everything under their feet.” In putting everything under them, God left nothing that is not subject to them. Yet at present we do not see everything subject to them.
putting everything in subjection under his feet.” Now in putting everything in subjection to him, he left nothing outside his control. At present, we do not yet see everything in subjection to him.
But there is a place where someone has testified: “What is mankind that you are mindful of them, a son of man that you care for him?
It has been testified somewhere, “What is man, that you are mindful of him, or the son of man, that you care for him?

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "We must pay the most careful attention to what we have heard, so that we do not drift away from it. Spiritual decline is usually gradual, not sudden—a slow drift, not a dramatic departure."

rec.title: John Calvin
rec.quote: "The first warning is framed as an argument from lesser to greater: if the angelic law carried penalties for disobedience, how much more terrible the consequence of neglecting the salvation proclaimed …"

rec.title: NET Bible translators
rec.quote: "The verb παραρυῶμεν (drift away) carries connotations of carelessness rather than intentional departure—the danger is neglect, not active rebellion."

debate: Psalm 8 was originally about humanity’s God-given dignity and dominion. The author of Hebrews reads it christologically: Jesus is the ‘son of man’ who…
Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads The Danger of Drifting and the Incarnate Son within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.
